result.markdown:
--- title: "Testing the robustness of a published claim: Teney (2016)" author: "Pair: [names]" date: today format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 2 code-fold: false df-print: kable execute: message: false warning: false knitr: opts_chunk: fig-width: 8 fig-height: 5 --- ## The claim, and what we are doing to it Teney (2016) studied how citizens frame the European Union. One claim from that paper is our target: > "… poor economic performances … decrease positive dimensions of EU framing" (Teney 2016: 619) Operationalised the way the Multi100 project constrained it for its five analysts: does a country's **unemployment rate** (poor economic performance) lower the **cosmopolitan** dimension of EU framing (peace, democracy, free movement, cultural diversity, a stronger say in the world)? The published anchor result was a coefficient of −0.0034, t = −4.03. You will hold the data, the tools, and the workflow the project used, and ask: how robust is that number to *justifiable* alternative choices? Work through the tasks below in order. Each is a small, committable step. ::: callout-tip ## How the tasks work Every task is a callout box with instructions, a **TODO** you complete, and code chunks that **already run** – so the report renders from the first preview. You edit the prose blanks and, in Tasks D2 and F, the code. Keep committing as you go. ::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ::: {.callout-note title="Task A – Concepts (the three Rs)"} **Before touching data, fix the vocabulary.** Four reanalysts walk into a seminar. Three of them are doing one of our three R's, while one is doing something else entirely**.** Label each with **reproducibility**, **robustness**, or **replicability** – and for the odd one out, say why none of the three quite fits. **TODO:** **Label each scenario and explain if needed** 1. \_\_\_ : A reviewer reruns the authors' Stata `.do` file on the authors' data and gets a *different* coefficient from the one printed in the paper. (Is the activity still named by what they *tried* to do, even though it failed?) 2. \_\_\_ : Same data, but the analyst re-implements the model in R instead of Stata and centres the predictor. Same conclusion. (Careful: is changing the *software* the same as changing the *analysis*?) 3. \_\_\_ : A preregistered team tests the identical hypothesis on a *separate, much larger* dataset chosen to be theoretically equivalent; and they get results that contradict those of the original analysis. 4. \_\_\_ : A different team tests the same underlying idea on social-media text using a machine-learning classifier, which is not a method used by the original analysis. So, what you will be doing in this exercise is... - This exercise is a \_\_\_ because our aim is to use \_\_\_ data \_\_\_ the original study and perform an analysis that is \_\_\_ the analysis done in the original study. When you have filled these in, commit: `git commit -am "Task A: define the three Rs"`. ::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ::: {.callout-note title="Task B – Grab the data from the web"} `R/get_data.R` fetches the published `rep_data.csv` from the Open Science Framework – the analyst's fork (OSF node `6zqct`) – with fallbacks. The file already ships in `data/`, so this is an OSF-access *exercise*: running it proves you can pull a real published artefact. If you are offline or the url-fetch gets blocked, it simply uses the local copy. **TODO:** run the chunks, then read the small-multiples figure. Which countries trend down over 2004–2013? Note one in a comment. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Fetch (or confirm) data/rep_data.csv. Prints which route it used. source("R/get_data.R") ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # The published country-year panel: 27 countries x 10 years = 270 rows. rep <- read_csv("data/rep_data.csv") glimpse(rep) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 270 Columns: 11 $ id "AT-2004", "AT-2005", "AT-2006", "AT-2007", "AT-2008", "… $ country "Austria", "Austria", "Austria", "Austria", "Austria", "… $ cntry "AT", "AT", "AT", "AT", "AT", "AT", "AT", "AT", "AT", "A… $ year 2004, 2005, 2006, 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010, 2011, 2012, 20… $ mposframe 0.4366, 0.4488, 0.4465, 0.4771, 0.4603, 0.4741, 0.4208, … $ mcosmopolitan 0.3644, 0.3680, 0.3594, 0.3892, 0.3811, 0.3927, 0.3504, … $ growth 2.7351, 2.2441, 3.4540, 3.7274, 1.4604, -3.7646, 1.8371,… $ unemp 5.83, 5.63, 5.24, 4.86, 4.13, 5.30, 4.82, 4.56, 4.87, 5.… $ N 416585, 416585, 416585, 416585, 416585, 416585, 416585, … $ n_cntry 16117, 16117, 16117, 16117, 16117, 16117, 16117, 16117, … $ n_cy 2059, 2020, 1017, 1011, 2003, 1003, 2000, 2019, 1986, 99… ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # How many countries, how many years, how many country-year cells? rep |> summarise( countries = n_distinct(cntry), years = n_distinct(year), rows = n() ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| countries| years| rows| |---------:|-----:|----:| | 27| 10| 270|
::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(rep, aes(x = year, y = mcosmopolitan)) + geom_line() + facet_wrap(~ cntry) + scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2004, 2008, 2013)) + labs(x = NULL, y = "Mean cosmopolitan framing (0–1)") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Mean cosmopolitan EU framing over time, by country – an echo of Teney's Figure 1.](index_files/figure-html/fig-cosmo-trend-1.png){#fig-cosmo-trend width=768} ::: ::: ::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ::: {.callout-note title="Task C – Estimand and DAG"} Before estimating, say *precisely* what you are estimating. An estimand has two parts: a **unit-specific quantity** and a **target population** (Lundberg et al. 2021). State it outside any model. **TODO – complete the estimand sentence:** > Unit-specific quantity: the change in \[\_\_\_\_\] for a one-point difference in \[\_\_\_\_\]. Target population: \[\_\_\_\_\]. *(A defensible filling: the change in mean cosmopolitan framing within a country-year, for a one-percentage-point difference in that country's unemployment rate; across EU member states, 2004–2013.)* Now the identification assumptions. `R/dag_starter.R` builds a half-drawn DAG: the causal path and two confounder pairs are in; the more arguable arrows are listed as ready-to-paste lines. Open the file, uncomment any edges you believe, re-source it, and re-run `adjustmentSets()`. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} source("R/dag_starter.R") plot_dag(dag) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![The starter DAG: unemployment → framing, with country and year as confounders.](index_files/figure-html/dag-1.png){width=768} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # What must we adjust for to identify the unemployment -> framing effect? dagitty::adjustmentSets(dag, exposure = "unemployment", outcome = "framing") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` { country, year } ``` ::: ::: The starter graph returns `{ country, year }`. That is the whole point of the two-way fixed effects you will fit next: country fixed effects adjust for `country`, year fixed effects adjust for `year`. **TODO – answer two questions in a sentence each:** 1. What do **country** and **year** fixed effects adjust for, in plain terms? *(country-constant traits, and shocks common to all countries in a year)* 2. What can they **not** fix? *(a time-varying, country-level confounder – e.g. GDP growth moving both unemployment and framing within a country over time)* Commit: `git commit -am "Task C: estimand + DAG + adjustment set"`. ::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ :::: {.callout-note title="Task D1 – Reproduce the constrained result"} Fit the Multi100-constrained model: cosmopolitan framing on (mean-centred) unemployment, with country and year fixed effects. We fit it **two ways** – with `plm` (what the published analyst used) and with `fixest::feols` (faster, and what the live demo uses). They should agree; that they do is itself a small robustness lesson. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Mean-centre unemployment, as the published analysis did. rep <- rep |> mutate(unemp_c = unemp - mean(unemp, na.rm = TRUE)) # plm: the published estimator. m_plm <- plm( mcosmopolitan ~ unemp_c, data = rep, index = c("cntry", "year"), effect = "twoways", model = "within" ) # fixest: the same model, fixed effects after the | . m_feols <- feols(mcosmopolitan ~ unemp_c | cntry + year, data = rep) ``` ::: ::: {#tbl-rep .cell tbl-cap='The constrained model on the published rep_data.csv, two estimators.'} ```{.r .cell-code} modelsummary( list("plm (two-way FE)" = m_plm, "fixest (two-way FE)" = m_feols), estimate = "{estimate}", statistic = "t = {statistic}", gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared"), coef_rename = c("unemp_c" = "Unemployment (centred)") ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
plm (two-way FE) fixest (two-way FE)
Unemployment (centred) -0.003 -0.003
t = -3.853 t = -3.853
Num.Obs. 270 270
R2 0.060 0.768
``` ::: ::: You just recovered **t = −3.853** on `rep_data.csv`. ::: {.callout-important title="Why the discrepancy?"} The result recorded for this analyst (C6HJR) in the Multi100 dataset is **t = −3.804**, not −3.853. Why is that? The original data-prep script read the EB 70.1 file (ZA4819) twice by mistake: once for 2008 (correct) and again in place of EB 72.4 (ZA4994) for 2009 (incorrect). **The analyst spotted this during the project and corrected it.** The recorded result came from the corrected pipeline. The annotation is preserved in the analyst's fork (`osf.io/6zqct`, folder `moreh_rep_vAPR2025/`): the script carries the note "APRIL 2025 – Mistake in dataset here; the dataset associated with 'eb724' should be 'ZA4994_v3-0-0.dta'". The official Multi100 component (`osf.io/8rtwe`) preserved only the earlier, uncorrected folder; the fork publishes the full trail. Coding can go wrong - even during a reanalysis! Keeping materials open and well documented helps spot any mistakes and correct them. That's the purpose of reproducibility. ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # The corrected panel: same construction, the right 2009 wave. ten <- read_csv("data/teney_panel.csv") |> mutate(unemp_c = unemp - mean(unemp, na.rm = TRUE)) m_corrected <- feols(mcosmo ~ unemp_c | cntry + year, data = ten) ``` ::: ::: {#tbl-corrected .cell tbl-cap='The same constrained model on the corrected teney_panel.csv.'} ```{.r .cell-code} modelsummary( list("corrected (fixest)" = m_corrected), estimate = "{estimate}", statistic = "t = {statistic}", gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared"), coef_rename = c("unemp_c" = "Unemployment (centred)") ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
corrected (fixest)
Unemployment (centred) -0.003
t = -3.804
Num.Obs. 270
R2 0.754
``` ::: ::: On the corrected panel the coefficient is **−0.00347** with **t = −3.804** – exactly the value recorded for analyst C6HJR in the Multi100 dataset. The constrained result finally reproduces. Now make it your own. Commit: `git commit -am "Task D1: reproduce the constrained result (t = -3.80)"`. :::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ::: {.callout-note title="Task D2 – Your analytical path (preregister, then run)"} Pick **one** deviation from the specification menu on the workshop site ([the spec menu](https://codemoreh.github.io/applied-replication/exercise/spec-menu.html)) – a different outcome scale, a different unemployment transform, adding GDP growth, a different estimator, a different sample window, or weights. Every option is *justifiable*; the discipline is choosing **with a reason**. **TODO – write your choice and a two-sentence justification:** > **Our spec:** \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ > > **Why (2 sentences):** \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ **Preregister it – this is the point of the task.** Commit your choice *before* you run it, with a message in this exact shape: ``` sh git commit -am "prereg: ~ | | " ``` *Only then* fill in the parameters below and run. The skeleton is a worked `fixest` example you adapt – change the variables, not the plumbing. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --- your choices (edit these) -------------------------------------------- my_outcome <- "mcosmo" # e.g. "mcosmo" (constrained) or "mpos" (all positive) my_exposure <- "unemp_c" # e.g. "unemp_c", "unemp", "log(unemp)" my_controls <- character(0) # e.g. "growth", or character(0) for none my_fe <- c("cntry", "year") # e.g. c("cntry","year"), or just "cntry" # --- build the formula and fit (you can leave this plumbing as is) --------- rhs <- paste(c(my_exposure, my_controls), collapse = " + ") fml <- as.formula( paste(my_outcome, "~", rhs, "|", paste(my_fe, collapse = " + ")) ) my_model <- feols(fml, data = ten) my_model ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` OLS estimation, Dep. Var.: mcosmo Observations: 270 Fixed-effects: cntry: 27, year: 10 Standard-errors: IID Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|) unemp_c -0.003473 0.000913 -3.804 0.00018214 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 RMSE: 0.034875 Adj. R2: 0.715885 Within R2: 0.058465 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Build your one-click submission link to the class-results form. source("R/report_result.R") report_result( my_model, spec = "mcosmo ~ unemp_c | twoway-FE | all 2004–2013" # describe YOUR spec ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Your result: spec = mcosmo ~ unemp_c | twoway-FE | all 2004–2013 b = -0.00347 | se = 0.00091 | t = -3.804 | df = 233 | n = 270 | r = -0.242 Submit (opens the form with your numbers pre-filled - just press Send): https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSezoAEOnZUfP4pkfN28_XtEBwSyc2RLsXB-h8RPSegF7_t76A/viewform?usp=pp_url&entry.832496914=mcosmo%20~%20unemp_c%20%7C%20twoway-FE%20%7C%20all%202004%E2%80%932013&entry.1656311800=-0.00347&entry.1337458262=0.00091&entry.1940779437=-3.804&entry.1682711955=233&entry.1586392724=270&entry.1660273001=-0.242 ``` ::: ::: Run this, then click the submission link it prints – your result lands as a dot on the live [Multiverse](https://codemoreh.github.io/applied-replication/results.html) chart. (Do it now, or in Task E.) ::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ::: {.callout-note title="Task E – Publish and share"} Get your report online and your result onto the live chart. 1. **Render** one last time: `quarto render` (or just save while `quarto preview` is running). 2. **Commit** your work: in Positron's **Source Control** panel, stage the changes, write a message (e.g. `Task E: final render and result`), and commit. 3. **Push**: click **⋯ → Push** (or the sync arrows) in Source Control. 4. **Submit your result** (if you have not already): run the `report_result()` chunk above and click the link it prints – your dot appears on the live [Multiverse](https://codemoreh.github.io/applied-replication/results.html) chart. 5. **Share your repo:** post your repository URL to the [Discussion gallery](https://github.com/CodeMoreh/applied-replication/discussions). 6. If you enabled Pages (Settings → Pages → Source: "GitHub Actions"), your live report appears at `https://.github.io/replication-lab/` within a minute or two of the push. Tick the passport boxes in `README.md` as you complete them – with a commit, of course. ::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ::: {.callout-note title="Task F – Stretch (optional)"} Time to spare? Pick one. The Teney (2016) paper went through all three Rs in the SCORE programme – and got three different verdicts. The same paper's push-button reproduction **failed** (the author-provided Stata code referenced file names that did not match what was available for download), and its preregistered secondary-data replication returned a **sign flip** (a positive coefficient, b = 0.002, t = 2.004, in the opposite direction to the original). You can explore the full SCORE dossier – reproduction, robustness, and replication side by side – at (SCORE parent node for this paper). - **A second spec.** Change `my_outcome`, `my_exposure`, or `my_fe` above, rerun, and `report_result()` it. Two points on the class multiverse instead of one. - **Fixed vs random effects.** Fit the random-effects version with `plm` and compare with a Hausman test – if it rejects, the fixed-effects estimate is the safer one. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Set eval: true to run. Compares within (FE) vs random effects. re <- plm(mcosmo ~ unemp_c, data = ten, index = c("cntry", "year"), effect = "twoways", model = "random") fe <- plm(mcosmo ~ unemp_c, data = ten, index = c("cntry", "year"), effect = "twoways", model = "within") phtest(fe, re) ``` ::: - **Weight by sample size.** Country-years rest on very different respondent counts (`n_cy`); weight the model by `n_cy` and see whether the estimate moves. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} feols(mcosmo ~ unemp_c | cntry + year, data = ten, weights = ~ n_cy) ``` ::: - **The mirror-image expectation.** The claim is about *positive* framing. Look at a **negative** dimension instead (`mneg`, or communitarian `mcomm`): if poor economic performance lowers positive framing, does it *raise* negative framing? Check the sign. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} feols(mneg ~ unemp_c | cntry + year, data = ten) ``` ::: - **New data + new analysis?** You could even attempt to import some new country-level variables to test alongside the ones available in the dataset – for example, importing data from the World Bank, for which there is even a dedicated `R` package (see [https://vincentarelbundock.github.io/WDI/](https://vincentarelbundock.github.io/WDI/)) –, or download data for more recent years from the Eurobarometer to test for *generalisability*. ::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## References Teney, C. (2016). Does the EU economic crisis undermine subjective Europeanization? Assessing the dynamics of citizens' EU framing between 2004 and 2013. *European Sociological Review*, 32(5), 619–633. Lundberg, I., Johnson, R., & Stewart, B. M. (2021). What is your estimand? Defining the target quantity connects statistical evidence to theory. *American Sociological Review*, 86(3), 532–565. Aczel, B., et al. (2026). The robustness of social science findings to analytical variability (Multi100). *Nature*, 652, 135–142.
